inline_comments.message: occurs to me - it could just throw an exception.  That would cause a non-zero exit without causing sonar complaints or hassles in junit tests.
maybe move this to an @After method

Another option is to add the below dependancy
       <dependency>
            <groupId>com.github.stefanbirkner</groupId>
            <artifactId>system-rules</artifactId>
            <version>1.19.0</version>
            <scope>test</scope>
        </dependency>
maybe to parent pom since we will need this in all repos, and then in test classes,
define a Rule:
    @Rule
    public final ExpectedSystemExit exit = ExpectedSystemExit.none();
and in each test case where System.exit is expected,add
    exit.expectSystemExitWithStatus(1);

I defined the SecurityManager and did the current way to avoid adding these kind of dependencies.

One thought is that we could differentiate error codes depending on the type of issue.   For example for DB errors, do a System.exit(1), for the configuration issue above, System.exit(2).  The application error codes themselves can be standardize across policy projects in policy/common in an enumeration or constants class, to try to have consistency on their meaning.

and yet another option is to wrap System.exit() in a method and then override the method in the junits so that it doesn't actually invoke exit().  The dummy method could set a flag with the exit status so that the tests could verify the value.  This avoids the permission manager altogether

Ok, I didn't know that. I can test that and verify and then do it that way. I agree System.exit is not the right choice for sure if we are not concerned about specific exitCodes.
How about Jorge's comment about specific exitCodes  specific to error types? If that is needed, then we will have to stick to System.exit I think.
We could have a specific exception in policy/common that has the enumeration in it.

Usage of System.exit is avoided in the latest patch by just throwing an exception. This also returns exitCode as 1.
If we are not returning exitCodes specifically, then should we really go with defining specific exceptions? Because, whatever exceptions we throw, the container will just return exitCode as 1, and then to know the error in detail we have to anyway go to the logs.
